3: A type: God's resting on the seventh day (Genesis 2:2) is the primordial type of the divine rest into which believers are invited to enter. The creation Sabbath is not merely the weekly day of rest but the pattern of the eschatological participation in God's own completed-work rest. Faith enters into what God completed at creation; the wilderness generation's failure was their refusal to enter what had been available since the foundation of the world. Christ opens the way into the rest that the creation Sabbath established as the pattern and goal.
14: A direct revelation: we have a great high priest who has passed through the heavens, Jesus the Son of God. The twofold identity — "Jesus" (his human name, bearer of the human condition) and "Son of God" (his divine identity) — defines the mediator who has already crossed the threshold of the heavenly sanctuary on the community's behalf. His passing through the heavens is not his departure from the community but his entry into the heavenly holy of holies where he intercedes for them.
15: A direct revelation: we have a high priest who is not unable to sympathize with our weaknesses, having been tested in every respect as we are, yet without sin. The sympathy of the Son is not generic divine knowledge of human experience but the specific, lived acquaintance with weakness and temptation that the incarnation gives him. The "yet without sin" is not a distance but a completion — the test was real, and the faithfulness was real; having endured every test, he can help those being tested from within the experience of having gone through them.
1: A theme: the promise of entering his rest still stands open. The rest that was forfeited by the wilderness generation has not been voided — it remains available through Christ who is both the rest-giver (his completed work is the basis of the rest) and the forerunner who has already entered the heavenly sanctuary as the community's pioneer. The exhortation to fear falling short is directed at those for whom the rest is genuinely available — those who have the Son.
2: A theme: the good news was proclaimed to them as to us, but they did not combine it with faith. The same gospel structure — divine promise, human reception, faith-response — applies to both the wilderness generation and the letter's community. The structural parallel reveals that salvation has always worked through the same mechanism: promise received by faith. Christ is the content of the promise that was proclaimed from the beginning; the wilderness generation's failure was not that they had the wrong promise but that they heard it without faith.
4: A type: God rested on the seventh day from all his works — the creation Sabbath is the primordial type of the eschatological rest that Christ opens. The rest into which faith enters is defined by the pattern of the creation Sabbath: completion of work (Genesis 2:2 = finished creation; Hebrews 10:12 = the Son sat down, finished sacrifice), followed by cessation. Christ's completed work is the antitype of the creation Sabbath; entering his rest is entering the completion that his sacrifice achieved.
5: A theme: "They shall never enter my rest" — the oath-of-exclusion placed over the wilderness generation reveals the stakes of the covenant-promise. The rest is real and guarded; those who reject it by unbelief are specifically excluded by divine oath. The oath that excluded the wilderness generation is the same oath whose positive counterpart (the oath backing the Melchizedekian priesthood of Psalms 110:4) secures the community's access through Christ.
6: A theme: some must still enter that rest, and the first generation's failure due to disobedience leaves it open. The logic of the still-open rest is Christological: the reason the rest remains available is that Christ has not yet completed the final gathering of all who will enter. The community addressed by Hebrews is the generation for whom Christ has opened the way; their entering completes what the wilderness generation left uncompleted.
7: A theme: God set a new "today" through David's Psalm — the ongoing scriptural "today" of Psalm 95 is the Spirit's continued opening of the entry-window. Each generation receives a fresh "today" because Christ's completed work is the eternal basis of the invitation. The chronological argument (David wrote Ps 95 after Joshua's Canaan-rest, proving the rest was still unfulfilled) points forward to the eschatological rest that Christ alone could open.
8: A type: if Joshua had given them rest, God would not have spoken of another day. Joshua is the OT type (Yehoshua = the same name as Jesus/Yeshua) whose partial, incomplete rest-giving points forward to the definitive rest-giving of Jesus. The type and antitype share the name as well as the pattern: the historical Joshua led Israel through the Jordan into Canaan; the eschatological Joshua/Jesus leads his people through death into the heavenly rest. The type's incompleteness proves it was always pointing beyond itself.
9: A direct revelation: there remains a Sabbath-rest for the people of God. The abiding nature of this rest is grounded in Christ's completed work: the "remains" is not an unfulfilled aspiration but the ongoing availability of what Christ has secured. The Sabbath-rest that "remains" is the rest into which the believing community enters now (the present dimension of Hebrews 4:3: "we who have believed are entering that rest") and will enter fully at the parousia.
10: A type: anyone who enters God's rest also rests from their own work, as God did from his. The cessation from self-generated effort is the positive content of faith-entry into Christ's rest: faith is the opposite of works as a means of approach to God. Christ's own completed work ("it is finished," John 19:30; he "sat down," Hebrews 10:12) is the pattern for the community's rest-from-self-effort — not moral passivity but the cessation of self-justifying striving in favor of Christ's sufficient achievement.
11: A theme: let us make every effort to enter that rest, so no one falls by the same pattern of disobedience. The effort to enter rest is the effort of faith — actively orienting toward Christ rather than drifting. The warning is addressed to those within the community of promise who are in danger of replicating the wilderness generation's faith-failure. The Christological content: the rest to be entered is Christ's rest; the disobedience to be avoided is the refusal to trust the one who opened it.
12: A theme: the living and active word of God that penetrates to the innermost human reality is the word that speaks in the Son (Hebrews 1:2). The word's penetrating power is not an impersonal force but the address of the living God who speaks to the community through his Son and his Spirit. The word's discriminating function — exposing the thoughts and intentions of the heart — is the necessary prelude to the merciful access to the throne of grace (v16). The word reveals what needs grace; the throne of grace provides it.
13: A theme: nothing is hidden from God's sight, and all must give account. The divine omniscience and judicial role that v13 asserts is the context within which the community approaches the throne of grace (v16). Before the judge, the community is exposed; through the high priest, the community is covered. The transition from v13 (exposed before God's eyes) to v14-16 (boldly approaching the throne of grace through the great high priest) is the movement from law to gospel, from judgment to mercy.
16: A direct revelation: let us approach the throne of grace with confidence, to receive mercy and find grace to help in time of need. The throne of grace is the heavenly court where the risen Christ permanently intercedes (Hebrews 7:25). The confidence to approach is grounded in his completed sacrifice and ongoing intercession — not in the community's own worthiness. The mercy and grace available at the throne is the content of the salvation that the Son has secured by his own entry into the most holy place.
